amendments.normalized_body: In `map_invoke_error` (service.rs:374-381), the promotion from `InvocationFailed{Timeout, details}` to `InvocationTimeout{backend, contract_id, timeout_ms}` silently discards the `details` string. This loses diagnostic context like "claude exceeded timeout of 600s" that was captured at the adapter level. Downstream log entries and error messages will only show the generic "backend invocation timed out" format. Consider either (a) preserving details in a new field on `InvocationTimeout`, or (b) logging the original details before promoting, so operators can still diagnose which binary timed out and see the adapter-level context.

The `classify_exit_failure` function (process_backend.rs:1449-1451) is a no-op that always returns `TransportFailure` regardless of the `ExitStatus` argument (the parameter is even named `_status`). While the comment explains the design rationale (exit codes after spawn are application-defined), this adds indirection with no behavior change. Either inline `FailureClass::TransportFailure` directly at the two call sites and remove the function, or implement the planned signal-based classification (e.g. SIGSEGV/SIGABRT) that the project prompt envisioned.

`src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1294-1307` now routes every non-zero child exit through `classify_exit_failure()`, but `src/adapters/process_backend.rs:1442-1450` is still a stub that always returns `FailureClass::TransportFailure`. That means exit 127 is still treated as retryable and will burn the expanded 5-attempt budget, even though shell-based wrappers commonly use 127 for `command not found`. A broken `claude`/`codex` wrapper that cannot exec a helper binary will therefore keep retrying instead of failing fast as intended.

`src/shared/error.rs:300-308` now maps every `AppError::BackendUnavailable` to `FailureClass::BinaryNotFound`, but `BackendUnavailable` is used for transient availability failures too. For example, `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:176-204` returns `BackendUnavailable` for HTTP client construction failures, probe request errors, and any non-2xx `/models` response, and `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:369-380` is called on every `AgentExecutionService::invoke()` via `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:166-169`. With this blanket remap, a transient OpenRouter 429/5xx or network hiccup is now classified as terminal `binary_not_found`, so the workflow never uses the new retry budget. The new non-retryable class needs to be assigned only at actual missing-binary/misconfiguration call sites, not by reclassifying the entire `BackendUnavailable` variant.

This loses diagnostic context like "claude exceeded timeout of 600s" that was captured at the adapter level. Downstream log entries and error messages will only show the generic "backend invocation timed out" format. Consider either (a) preserving details in a new field on `InvocationTimeout`, or (b) logging the original details before promoting, so operators can still diagnose which binary timed out and see the adapter-level context.

`src/shared/error.rs:300-308` now maps every `AppError::BackendUnavailable` to `FailureClass::BinaryNotFound`, but `BackendUnavailable` is used for transient availability failures too. For example, `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:176-204` returns `BackendUnavailable` for HTTP client construction failures, probe request errors, and any non-2xx `/models` response, and `src/adapters/openrouter_backend.rs:369-380` is called on every `AgentExecutionService::invoke()` via `src/contexts/agent_execution/service.rs:166-169`. With this blanket remap, a transient OpenRouter 429/5xx or network hiccup is now classified as terminal `binary_not_found`, so the workflow never uses the new retry budget. The new non-retryable class needs to be assigned only at actual missing-binary/misconfiguration call sites, not by reclassifying the entire `BackendUnavailable` variant.

payload.summary: Final review accepted 2 amendment(s); restart required.
